So I just think it's a combination of likely it's more SMB CEOs that are probably taking the survey and that they are likely AI forward people by nature and so they're going to have that kind of confidence level in their understanding and adoption.

How do you handle institutional resistance to AI?

46:36 · Someone asked how are you handling institutional resistance to AI and they gave some really interesting context here. They said, "I'm the AI subject matter expert for the company I work for, and I've been trying to increase adoption across departments, but I come across roadblocks either from poor communication around the topic of AI between managers and their direct reports or personal ethical concerns that people are using to qualify outright refusal to use the technology."

47:07 · Taylor, how do you look at this topic?

47:09 · Do we have any kind of data or metrics around this?

47:13 · So yeah, I think there's a lot of ways that you could think about this and a lot of potential, you know, like this person cited, a number of reasons behind the resistance. Um, one of the things that I dug into was who owns AI adoption within the company and how that might correlate to the momentum. So does ownership by certain people um lead to better outcomes, more uh widespread adoption and more success there? And um not surprisingly, overall dedicated AI leadership was the most likely to have um better AI adoption and kind of momentum without the the company. And also then following behind that was cross functional AI councils. So if you have someone dedicated to it, that is likely going to lead to um you know better more consistent progress and momentum within the organization. Um next up is executive leadership. So specifically if we're saying positive momentum, 64% um site positive momentum if they have dedicated AI leadership. 60% site uh positive momentum if they have executive leadership owning AI. Um at the bottom technology leadership so if it owns AI adoption only 47% say that they have positive momentum they are much more likely to say that they have this really inconsistent or siloed progress. So I think that really speaks to um what Paul talked about in his opening letter of the report that if this is something that is just handed off to you know legal or IT to solve it doesn't really get rolled out in the right way. Um whereas if there's a head of it a chief a um a head of AI a chief AI officer or you know the CEO is owning it you have that kind of top down authority to actually get things done. Couple of notes real quick, Mike. I would say it's critical to get through all these obstacles that you have to lead with empathy. You have to understand where employees are at and that some of them don't like AI. Some of them are afraid it's going to take their jobs. Some of them just don't have the time to learn it, but they want to. Like nobody wants to be irrelevant or obsolete in their career. So like you have to be able to meet people where they are. You have to have from a leadership perspective a clear vision for the future of work and what that means to your business. Like if you are mandating AI literacy and competency over the next 12 months, say that. If it's going to affect people's future employment at a company, say that like give them the reason why you're enabling all these things through, you know, actionable guidance on technology use through AI education and and training systems and then think of it as a communications and change management program. This is not a technology thing like technology is the core that's going to enable it but this is all about how you communicate it to the different people within your organization and then how you empower them through change management and how you're transparent about everything in that process. So the obstacles can't just be overcome with one thing or another. It is truly a comprehensive approach to transformation. And that's where most companies we talked to are are really struggling is they just treated it as a technology thing to be solved that we're just going to buy some licenses and give people the Genai app tools and it'll all work itself out. And that is not the way to go.

50:36 · Amen to that. Especially that communication piece of it, Paul. Like I'm biased just because we're, you know, communicators, writers, but I've just seen so many organizations struggle with that. It's like before you do anything else technology wise, bring someone in, have them talk to your organization like or get your communications in order. It can go such a long way.

50:55 · That's why we talked about up front. I think like marketing is often the tip of the spear when it comes to adoption within organizations is your marketing and comm's people. They've got to be able to work with HR and leadership to communicate this stuff to people internally. It's an internal communications plan. And if you're a big enterprise, you have an internal comm's team. They better have high degrees of AI literacy. like you can't have the communications people who control the message not understand the moment themselves. And so that's where we just we see this lack of like we use the term a lot in the podcast lack of situational awareness of the true scope of what has to happen. And I think everybody just wants the quick fix and it's just not the reality.

51:36 · Yeah.

51:36 · One last thing I would just add that I see a lot is that people fail to appreciate that the narrative environment is very different around this than many other things. Like if I go home at the end of the day and I haven't heard during work about our benefits plan, I'm not reading headlines about benefits plans. Like I'm not getting inundated with hype and misinformation and doom and gloom about benefits plans. I if you're not saying stuff about AI, your employees, your teams, they are hearing about it from somewhere else and it's probably not great. Yeah. Exactly. Right. [laughter] So, it's like really important to understand.

52:12 · Okay.

52:12 · Next question. How are you reconciling the challenge of teaching AI skills with folks who enter the conversation at such with such wildly diverse experiences and backgrounds. So I think Taylor's kind of comes from the fact that this data shows people are all over the place in terms of AI understanding, adoption, sophistication.

How do you teach AI skills to people at wildly different levels?

52:33 · We see this, all of us see this every day as speakers, instructors. It's crazy. You can go into a room of a hundred people and it's almost sometimes Taylor, I feel like we talk about this a lot. It's like they're in a hundred different places. Like how with AI, how do you reconcile all that?

52:51 · Yeah.

52:51 · I mean, I I think getting a baseline of where people are, to your point, is really important to figure out exactly who you're talking to and and at what level you're talking to them. Um, yeah, I don't know. It's hard. I mean, we something we talk about internally is like, how do you teach this when people are in such wildly different places? Um, I mean, one thing that comes to mind because we just did our AI for education course is the ability to provide, I guess, broader, more personalized learning and education to different people and meet them where they are, wherever they are in their AI journey and help them get to that next step is more possible than ever. So, that's certainly, I think, a piece of it is not having a one-sizefits-all kind of training plan. Um but finding those ways where you can identify your your more beginner, intermediate and advanced um users, your power users and start to meet people with different types of content and training um that matches where they are currently in their understanding and use of AI.

53:55 · Yeah, it's interesting. I also kind of flip that too and we've talked a lot about I think about like what doesn't change or what would what would everyone need to learn regardless of level right it's like I regardless of how good or bad I am with AI I already I know for a fact I'm going to need to find use cases how can I teach people to do that you know stuff like that it's not the whole ball game but you kind of either look at the hyperpersonalized side or look at the evergreen side in tandem I feel like yeah the timeless stuff for sure Yeah.

54:26 · Okay. For this next question, Paul, I'm going to throw this one at you as I know we've talked about this more and more on the weekly podcast. We Someone asked, "We are starting to get pressure from a handful of employees at this person's organization to measure AI usage and its impact on the environment. I don't know where this organization is based or what industry they're in, but they said then they are under pressure to purchase certificates to offset our impact." The question is, are you hearing more concerns about AI's environmental impact and how are companies addressing it?

How are companies addressing employee concerns about AI's environmental impact?

54:58 · We get asked questions about it all the time. I mean, anytime I go do a keynote somewhere, it's one of the first questions I'll get asked, whether it's water usage or drain, you know, energy usage, powering the data centers. Um, most people don't like the answer, which is there's just not a hell of a lot you can do at the moment. I mean the most for most people that are users of the technology which is going to be mandated as part of your job. Like I've said this and I I don't mean it to sound cold in any way at all. I'm like completely empathetic to this. It's why we have Karen how as a keynote at Makeon this year is like we're trying to actually raise awareness about the environmental impact.

55:36 · What I will tell people though is if you don't use AI and you work in basically any industry in any knowledge work role, you won't have a job in 3 to 5 years. So like not using it's literally like saying I I refuse to use a computer because like computers negatively impact the environment. Okay, that that's fine.

55:56 · Like you can take that position but you won't have a job that requires the use of a computer. And so I feel like there I can't come up with a single knowledge work role where AI won't be infused into what you're doing either in the hardware you use, the software you use, whatever it may be. It's just going to be there.

56:14 · So not using it because of your um feelings toward the environment it is a viable personal choice to make but it is not a good career choice to make and so then the question becomes okay but what can we do so there are probably things that an organizational level if you work in a bigger enterprise there's probably things that the IT team can do um specifically probably IT to think about the most efficient usage of the technology and then I think down to the individual level. The higher your a AI literacy, the more you understand about the different models you can choose to use, the better you are at prompting, the better you are providing proper context. So fewer prompts are needed to get the output you need. Like that is really the best way to minimize the impact on the environment at an individual level is to get really really good at using it. So like as a really practical example, if I go into Anthropic Claude today and I have a project I want to work on, I can choose from any of what in episode 218 I listed them. Any of like 12 models like they're all in the drop down.

57:21 · If I know that sonnet 4.6 or whatever the the sonnet current sonnet version is will do the job, then I'm going to do that. I'm going to use sonnet 4.6. I'm not going to try fable five to like help me write a strategic brief like I don't need it. So, I'm going to use far fewer tokens and then in the prompt I give it, I'm going to be really thoughtful upfront of like what exactly I want.

57:42 · Here's all the context you need so that the the AI can do its job as quickly as possible. That is that's it. Like to me that at a personal level, like I've used the analogy, I think I heard it from somebody else. It's like turning the lights off when you leave a room. Like we all want to save electricity, but the reality is we all use electricity every day. So, just don't waste it. Don't leave the water running in the sink.

58:03 · Like there's little That's basically where we're at with AI is be a um be a someone who understands how to use the tools as efficiently as possible so that you minimize the impact you're having on the environment. You maximize the value of the output you're creating.

58:20 · I love that.

Which industries have the greatest growth opportunity in the age of AI?

58:22 · Okay, Paul, another question I want to throw your way first. Um what industries do you believe have the greatest opportunity for growth in the age of AI?

58:30 · Do you see anything in your experience or in your research that's most likely to decline?

58:36 · This is one where I think it's it's very a personal like your answer to this is going to be very personal. You know, I think every industry you can look around and say there's ability to build a smarter version of a company. So like the name Smarter X, this is literally where it came from is I I years ago I was like you could just build a smarter version of every department, every company. And so the X became the variable of what industry you want to do, what company type do you want to do.

58:57 · And so that's what it was meant to be.

59:00 · And so if you're an attorney or if you're in HR or if you're in sales or if you're in manufacturing or like whatever it is you do, whatever industry you're in, there's an opportunity there for dramatic growth to build an AI native version of a company in that industry or to join a company that's trying to build an AI native version. And there's enormous uh total addressable markets in every industry to just infuse AI in responsible ways to that industry. So, I don't know that I look around and pick like one specific one. We're obviously very bullish on media companies cuz first and foremost, we are a media company. We create content and build an audience. I events, education, like I'm very very bullish. We're trying to reimagine what research looks like, trying to make it more real time, um, more dynamic, um, and like consulting services, advisory service, like I'm very bullish on all those things, but that's that's what we do. It's what I know. So, I would say whatever you do, um, there's there's growth potential in it.

59:58 · Okay, Taylor, I'm curious about your perspective on this one. We've got a couple final questions here. Do you think that AI will progress past the barriers identified in the report? And in your opinion, what do you think is the biggest driver to enable that to happen?

Will AI progress past the barriers identified in the report?

1:00:14 · So the thing that's interesting here um as far as AI progressing past the barriers I mean a lot of the barriers we identified weren't specific to things with the technology itself the models itself that somehow needs to change or be solved. I mean we when we asked um which are the top barriers to AI adoption people could choose up to three. 15% chose budget 10% chose tech failing to meet expectations 9% lack of the right data. So that was really toward the bottom of things that felt like they were somehow the technology was inadequate. The top barriers were lack of education and training, lack of awareness or understanding, lack of time, um fear or mistrust of AI. So 29% of people picked that. That's really the only one that feels like it implies maybe a failure of the tech itself. Like you could fe you're full of hallucinations or a lack of trust in the outputs. But really, I mean, the barriers we're talking about are um specific to people and building awareness, education, um trust, you know, change management. I mean, that is really, I think, more of what needs to happen more so than the technology itself somehow needing to be different. Um, so I think it kind of goes back to a lot of the things that, you know, we're talking about of communication and empathy and and training and um, being there for your people at this moment in time more so than, you know, Fable 5 solving all of our problems.

1:01:48 · All right. So, our final question here, I'd like to get both your perspectives.

What was the most surprising finding from this year's research?

1:01:52 · Maybe Taylor, you first and then Paul, you can close us out here. But from the report, what was the most surprising insight to you this year and why?

1:02:01 · So there was another question that we asked this year about their company's top outcome. What was their company's top outcome with AI? We've asked this in the past. Every year it has been productivity. Everybody wants productivity. And I just had this feeling that I wanted to um change the question because I mean you guys talk about this all the time that like we see the writing on the wall with with jobs with headcount with flat headcount. And so um we changed the options for this question this year to specifically define productivity as increasing output with the same resources and then we added decreasing operating costs either through reducing headcount or overhead.

1:02:44 · Now, my assumption was that, okay, productivity is not going to be the leader anymore. We're going to see some people saying that, you know, they're trying to reduce operating costs with AI. Like, that feels like it's at least somewhat directionally true. And I was totally wrong. Productivity was again at the top. Only 4% selected reducing operating costs. It was one of the lowest selected options of anything that we put on the survey. So, that alone was interesting. But then you also put it into the context of 71% believe that AI is going to cut jobs. So to me it felt like this weird disconnect of okay, so you do believe that jobs are going to be cut. That seems overwhelmingly true or at least overwhelmingly believed to be true, but nobody really thinks it's going to happen at their company and that's not what their intent and their goal is, but they think somebody else's goal is to do that. So there was just this weird disconnect of looking at these multiple questions um together and then you look at 20% are not concerned about their job but 71% believe jobs. So there just feels like there's this weird moment where there's this um I don't know if it's cognitive dissonance or if it's just you you believe that your company is different from how everybody else is going to act. And I thought that was a really interesting finding.

1:04:05 · Paul, how about you?

1:04:07 · You know, the jump in jobs I think is obviously a standout data. The perception of the negative impact on jobs. I'm not surprised by that though. Like I I was actually surprised how low it was last year at 53%. Um so I wouldn't say that's surprising to me.

1:04:23 · The thing that you know every year we watch this data and I wait for these these leaps like in you know number of people that now have AI education and training or the number of people that you know think that the impact on jobs is going to be negative. The one that jumped out to me is I think the first time we did this uh was this year where we actually looked at the four factors of governance and only 13% have all four of them. Um that's one to keep an eye on because I think that is so fundamental to properly scaling AI treating it as a true transformation initiative not just a technology project. Um, and so I I'll be really curious in future years to start watching that combined data point to see how many organizations really are doing all the necessary components to do AI in a responsible way.

1:05:08 · Awesome. Well, just a quick reminder for everyone as we wrap up here, go to stateofbusiness.ai to check out the full report. Tons tons more in there. Taylor, incredible job on this this year. It's such a such a privilege to work a little bit with you on this and just see the end result. And of course, thank you for answering all these awesome questions from our audience. Paul, thank you for doing the same. Really appreciate it.

1:05:30 · Yeah, thanks, Mike. And I think we may be uh seeing Taylor again on the podcast sometime soon. So, for sure. [laughter] Happy to come back.

1:05:37 · All right. Thank you.

1:05:39 · Thanks for listening to AI Answers. To keep learning, visit smarterx.ai where you'll find on demand courses, [music] upcoming classes, and practical resources to guide your AI journey. And if you've got a question for a future episode, we'd love to [music] hear it. That's it for now. Continue exploring and keep asking great questions about AI.

item.title: Bloomberg Originals - Inside Anthropic, the $965 Billion AI Juggernaut | The Circuit
item.description: Anthropic, a company founded by former OpenAI defectors, has rapidly ascended to become a leading force in the artificial intelligence landscape. Valued at nearly a trillion dollars, Anthropic is helm...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Inside Anthropic, the $965 Billion AI Juggernaut | The Circuit

Anthropic: The AI Underdog Aiming for Global Impact

Anthropic, a company founded by former OpenAI defectors, has rapidly ascended to become a leading force in the artificial intelligence landscape. Valued at nearly a trillion dollars, Anthropic is helmed by a sibling duo, Dario and Daniela Amodei, who are navigating the complex terrain of AI development, safety, and societal impact. While Dario serves as the visionary, Daniela acts as the operator, translating his ambitious ideas into tangible progress.

From Underdog to Frontrunner

Founded in 2021, Anthropic began as an underdog lab with a mission to build responsible AI for the long-term benefit of humanity. This mission is reflected in the company's name, derived from the Greek word for "human." Today, Anthropic is a breakout star, challenging established players and making significant waves in the tech industry. The company's rapid development cycle is powered by its own AI, Claude, which is integrated across the product development process, enabling swift releases.

The Amodei Vision: Safety and Responsibility

The Amodei siblings share a deep-seated desire to create something significant together. Their upbringing in San Francisco, with a father who was a leather craftsman and a mother who worked in libraries, instilled in them a sense of curiosity and a love for learning. While Dario was drawn to math and understanding the universe, Daniela was more inclined towards reading and the arts, creating a complementary dynamic.

Dario's early academic pursuits in neuroscience led him to AI, where he made significant contributions at Baidu and Google. Daniela, after an early stint at Stripe, joined him at OpenAI. It was at OpenAI, initially founded as a non-profit aiming for safe and open superintelligence, that Dario developed the concept of "scaling laws," predicting that larger models with more data and computing power would inherently become smarter.

However, disagreements over the company's direction and values led Dario and Daniela to leave OpenAI. Dario has stated that the decision to leave stemmed from a lack of trust and a divergence in values, rather than solely safety concerns. This led to the founding of Anthropic, with the early team often gathering in San Francisco's Precita Park to discuss their vision. Notably, Anthropic has retained all of its co-founders, a rarity in the fast-paced tech world.

Claude: The AI with a "Professional Warmth"

Anthropic's flagship AI model, Claude, is designed with a distinct personality, aiming for "professional warmth." The goal is not for Claude to be a best friend, but rather approachable yet distant, avoiding the cold, rote calculation often associated with AI. This persona is cultivated through a set of principles known as a "constitution," intended to guide Claude's behavior.

A key aspect of Claude's training involves teaching it to be "good," which means avoiding lies (or "hallucinations," where the model invents information) and deception. Harmlessness is another critical component, ensuring Claude doesn't inadvertently produce harmful or incorrect information. The challenge lies in defining "good" and "harmless" across diverse human values. Anthropic draws upon foundational human documents like the UN Declaration of Human Rights and engages with religious leaders to incorporate universally consistent values into Claude's character.

Early iterations of Claude sometimes exhibited an overly "nannyish" tendency, expressing excessive concern for benign requests. The development process is described as "tuning a dial," a delicate balance researchers strive to achieve.

Business Strategy: Enterprise Over Entertainment

Anthropic's focus on enterprise solutions, particularly in coding and business applications, has been a strategic differentiator. Claude Code automates significant portions of software engineering, while Claude Cowork extends this power to a broader user base. This approach has contributed to Anthropic's profitability and rapid revenue growth.

Dario Amodei explains this focus as a deliberate choice to align business models with their values. Unlike consumer-facing applications that might incentivize engagement and addiction, enterprise solutions aim to make AI genuinely useful. This aligns with Anthropic's broader goals of using AI to advance scientific research, cure diseases, and improve energy efficiency. This strategic decision has positioned Anthropic to weather industry shifts, such as the "SaaSpocalypse" where billions in market value vanished overnight for some software stocks.

The Engineer's New Superpowers

The development of Claude Code and Claude Cowork was spearheaded by engineer Boris Cherny, who joined Anthropic after a period of living a quiet life in rural Japan. Inspired by the transformative potential of AI and a deep understanding of its risks from science fiction, Cherny was drawn back to the field.

Claude Code represents a significant leap beyond simple auto-completion, with the ambition of creating a true coding agent. Engineers at Anthropic report that Claude is writing almost all of the code for their teams, with some personally relying on it for 100% of their coding needs for months. This has fundamentally changed the engineering experience, making it more fun and empowering, akin to having "superpowers."

A demonstration of Claude's capabilities involved creating a recipe app with meal suggestions for the week. What once took hours or days for developers was accomplished by Claude in minutes, highlighting the immense opportunity AI presents.

The AI Job Market Debate

The rapid advancement of AI has ignited a fierce debate about its impact on jobs. While Silicon Valley buzzes with AI innovation, a significant portion of the public remains anxious, with 70% of Americans believing AI will kill jobs. Dario Amodei has been vocal about this concern, predicting a future of high GDP growth alongside high unemployment or underemployment. He has stated that AI could eliminate half of all entry-level white-collar jobs within one to five years, a concern that remains potent.

Amodei acknowledges the "usual hump" where automation increases productivity, but eventually approaches full automation. He emphasizes that while AI currently makes software engineers more productive, there's a point where the AI itself becomes more efficient than human intervention. This prospect is described as "very uncomfortable."

Anthropic's mission, for many of its employees, is to steer this powerful force towards a better outcome. Amodei believes this is a societal challenge that requires advocacy and discussion, extending beyond any single company.

Navigating Criticism and Geopolitics

Anthropic faces criticism, with some accusing them of "doom marketing" that benefits the company. Jensen Huang of Nvidia has argued that Amodei conflates tasks with jobs, asserting that AI is creating new roles. Amodei pushes back against this, emphasizing that his warnings are carefully articulated in his writings, distinguishing between tasks and jobs and explaining why this technological shift is different from past industrial revolutions. He views the criticism of his warnings as a symptom of Silicon Valley's short-attention-span culture.

Beyond the domestic impact, Anthropic is deeply involved in the geopolitical implications of AI. Dario Amodei has been outspoken about the need for export controls on AI chips to China, viewing China's advancement in AI as a threat to democracy. He acknowledges that this stance may displease chipmakers and even partners, but maintains it stems from his core beliefs.

AI in Warfare: A Moral Tightrope

Anthropic's involvement with the US Department of Defense highlights the complex ethical landscape of AI. Despite Dario's longstanding anti-war stance, Anthropic signed a contract to operate on classified networks used for warfare. Amodei explains this decision as a response to a changing world, citing the aggression of authoritarian regimes and the need for national defense.

The company's partnership with Palantir, whose technology is used by agencies like ICE, has raised questions about potential surveillance applications. Anthropic maintains strict policies against working with agencies like ICE or CBP and denies involvement in Gaza.

A significant turning point occurred when Anthropic, along with other AI leaders, secured a $200 million contract with the Pentagon. However, the relationship soured when the Department of Defense demanded full use of Anthropic's AI technology without guardrails. Anthropic refused to allow Claude to be used for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons, leading to a blacklisting by the Pentagon. This conflict underscored the tension between commercial interests and military applications, with critics labeling Anthropic's leadership as "ideological lunatics."

Amodei frames this as a debate about the proper use of AI by the government, emphasizing the need to establish precedents for both beneficial and concerning use cases. While acknowledging that AI can increase military efficiency, he stresses that human decision-making must remain paramount. The incident involving a missile strike in Iran, where Claude may have assisted in targeting, brought these concerns to the forefront. Anthropic maintains that human oversight was in place, but the potential for AI to accelerate conflict remains a grave concern.

Mythos: The "Superweapon" AI

A new AI model, Mythos, has emerged from Anthropic, described as a "superweapon" due to its ability to identify thousands of cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Anthropic believes this model poses an enormous threat if fully released, capable of hacking banks, accessing state secrets, and crippling critical infrastructure. Early recipients of Mythos described it as a tool that should require a "gun license."

Through Project Glasswing, Anthropic provided select organizations, including the National Security Administration, with access to Mythos. This initiative aims to ensure that "good guys" have the tools to defend themselves, anticipating that adversaries will eventually develop similar capabilities. The company faces criticism for deciding who gets access to such powerful technology, but maintains that these decisions are grounded in specific fears, particularly around cybersecurity.

The Future of AI Governance

The development of powerful AI like Mythos raises profound questions about governance. Amodei expresses concern about both private companies and governments controlling such technology, advocating for a sensible, moderate approach to regulation. He believes that pre-release testing and auditing of AI models should be mandatory.

The debate over regulation has seen shifts, with some initially advocating for minimal government intervention now calling for nationalization of AI technology. Amodei criticizes this "yo-yoing" between extremes, emphasizing Anthropic's consistent stance favoring a balanced approach.

The Weight of the Moment

The intense reaction to AI, marked by both excitement and anxiety, is palpable. Anthropic has cultivated a loyal following, but also faces protests and confusion. Amodei acknowledges the weight of this moment, recognizing that the risks may outweigh the benefits for many. He expresses a hope for the best but plans for the worst, viewing his work at Anthropic as the most important of his career.

The personal impact of the AI race has become evident, with Sam Altman's home being attacked. This event has heightened concerns about rhetoric leading to negative outcomes and underscores the need for peaceful debate. Amodei sees this as a "less savory aspect of the exponential," where increased societal impact leads to greater attention and potential backlash.

Learning from Social Media's Mistakes

The possibility of AI being banned, similar to social media, is acknowledged. Amodei believes that if social media companies could revisit their past, they would act differently, learning from challenges related to child welfare, mental health, and election integrity. Anthropic's mission is to proactively address potential risks, rather than reactively justify them.

The company's identity is deeply intertwined with its commitment to doing things right and prioritizing AI safety. Amodei hopes to get it right the first time, avoiding the scramble to justify mistakes.

The Existential Stakes

The potential impacts of AI are seen as existential, with some viewing it as the "end of work." Amodei believes Anthropic has a responsibility to cushion the blow for those whose lives are upended by this technology. He rejects the notion that companies should simply focus on product growth without considering the societal consequences.

Trust and Responsibility

When asked why the public should trust Anthropic, Amodei acknowledges that starting from a position of distrust is rational, given Silicon Valley's past. He asserts that Anthropic is different and that this difference must be earned through actions.

Drawing parallels to the development of the atomic bomb, Amodei identifies more with Leo Szilard, who foresaw the chain reaction, than with Oppenheimer. He views Oppenheimer as a cautionary tale, emphasizing the need for checks and balances rather than relying on larger-than-life personalities.

Amodei's estimate of a 10-25% chance of civilization collapse is a stark reminder of the stakes. He attributes this probability not to Anthropic's technology specifically, but to the confluence of powerful technology, multiple nations, and competing companies. Anthropic's work is dedicated to reducing this risk, though it can never be zero. He uses the analogy of an airline company: while one can strive for significantly greater safety, guaranteeing zero crashes is impossible. The goal is to drastically lower the probability of catastrophic failure.

Finding Zen Amidst the AI Revolution

In moments of intense pressure, Amodei finds relaxation through various means, including playing video games, traveling to Italy, and simply observing nature, like his horse Calypso, who remains blissfully unaware of the complex world of AI.

item.title: CNBC International - 143 Investors Said No. Now WHOOP Is Worth $10 Billion
item.description: At just 22 years old, with no prior entrepreneurial experience or full-time job, Will Ahmed embarked on building a company at the complex intersection of hardware, software, data science, and medicine...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: 143 Investors Said No. Now WHOOP Is Worth $10 Billion

From Skepticism to $10.1 Billion: The WHOOP Story

At just 22 years old, with no prior entrepreneurial experience or full-time job, Will Ahmed embarked on building a company at the complex intersection of hardware, software, data science, and medicine. Facing widespread skepticism due to his lack of specialized engineering background, he encountered rejection from hundreds of investors. Yet, his daily commitment to perseverance fueled the creation of WHOOP, a company now valued at $10.1 billion and adopted by elite athletes worldwide.

The Genesis of an Idea

Ahmed's journey began in 2012 while he was a student at Harvard. His obsession with sports and technology, honed through playing ten different sports as a child and eventually captaining the Harvard squash team, led him to confront a personal challenge: overtraining. He experienced cycles of intense fitness followed by sudden, unexplained exhaustion, a phenomenon he found deeply frustrating and personally betraying.

This personal struggle sparked a broader question: why, in an age where we have detailed information about weather, computers, and cars, do we know so little about our own bodies, arguably the most critical "engine" we possess? This question evolved into an obsession, prompting a 20-year-old Ahmed to delve into hundreds of medical papers and write his own research paper titled "The Feedback Tool: Measuring Intensity Recovery and Sleep."

A Leap of Faith

In the fall of his senior year at Harvard, Ahmed made the pivotal decision to forgo traditional career paths in finance or consulting, opting instead to launch his own company. This was a daunting prospect, marked by significant uncertainty and a lack of a clear playbook. He was stepping into his first full-time job as a founder, venturing into uncharted territory.

Ahmed's vision for WHOOP was not to create another step counter. He aimed to develop a wearable device capable of continuously tracking internal bodily metrics, including sleep, strain, and recovery. This ambitious goal meant combining the user-friendliness of a consumer wearable with the sophisticated physiological data typically found in clinical or elite performance settings.

Navigating Skepticism and Competition

The early days were fraught with challenges. The wearables market in 2012 was nascent, with only about five million units sold annually. Most existing products offered superficial, non-invasive metrics, failing to capture the body's internal physiological state. Ahmed's pursuit of deep physiological insights was met with resistance, leading to rejection from hundreds of investors.

Adding to the pressure, WHOOP entered a competitive landscape that included giants like Nike, with its FuelBand, and the impending launch of the Apple Watch. A prevailing narrative suggested that WHOOP should focus solely on software and algorithms, leveraging existing wearable platforms rather than building its own hardware. However, Ahmed believed this perspective overlooked a critical gap: the absence of any wearable technology capable of accurately measuring the human body. He persisted, dedicating years to developing a product he wouldn't be embarrassed by, striving for a level of perfection in its design and functionality.

The Elite Athlete Strategy

Once a viable product was developed, Ahmed made another strategic, counterintuitive decision: to target elite athletes first, rather than the mass consumer market. He reasoned that professional athletes, whose careers and earnings depended heavily on their recovery, had a direct need for measurable insights into sleep and recovery. Furthermore, he believed that gaining the endorsement and loyalty of these high-profile individuals would be instrumental in building a brand around performance and making health monitoring aspirational and "cool."

This strategy proved effective. High-profile athletes like LeBron James and Michael Phelps became early adopters, and WHOOP began to spread organically through word-of-mouth within athletic communities.

The Brink of Bankruptcy and a Founder's Crisis

Despite the growing brand recognition and powerful technology, building a sustainable business proved immensely challenging. By 2017-2018, WHOOP had cultivated a strong brand and was embraced by top athletes, but it had not yet achieved mass adoption or profitability. Investors began to question the business's viability, and the company found itself on the verge of bankruptcy, just a week away from closing its doors before securing a crucial deal.

The immense pressure of leading a rapidly growing company took a personal toll on Ahmed. At 24, having raised tens of millions of dollars and managing a team of 30-40 people, he felt overwhelmed. His stress manifested in excessive coffee and alcohol consumption, leading to a panic attack that necessitated hospitalization. This critical moment forced a stark realization: WHOOP's failure could stem not from a flawed idea or technology, but from his own inability to evolve and lead at the pace the business demanded. He understood that if he didn't grow and adapt, the business's fate would be his responsibility.

A Business Model Pivot

Following his hospitalization, Ahmed committed to personal growth, incorporating meditation and stress management techniques into his routine, and consciously adopting the leadership style WHOOP required. However, personal transformation alone wasn't enough; the business itself needed a fundamental shift.

In May 2018, Ahmed made a decisive move that redefined WHOOP's business model: he eliminated the one-time hardware purchase price. The WHOOP strap became free, bundled with a monthly membership. This pivot recognized that health monitoring is an ongoing journey, not a singular event. The subscription model allowed for continuous investment in software features and capabilities, aligning the company's revenue with the continuous value it provided to its members. This approach fostered a strong focus on member retention and a deep understanding of their evolving needs.

Expanding Horizons: From Fitness to Healthspan

Today, Ahmed envisions WHOOP as more than just a fitness tracker, seeing the blurring lines between performance and health. The company is increasingly focused on delivering comprehensive health insights, including health alerts and even medical capabilities, recognizing that optimal health is intrinsically linked to peak performance.

In May 2025, WHOOP launched WHOOP 5.0 and WHOOP MG, introducing features like "WHOOP Age" (measuring physiological age based on behaviors and physiology) and "Heart Screener" with ECG and Blood Pressure Insights. The popularity of WHOOP Age, becoming the most screenshotted feature in the app, highlights a strong consumer desire for control over their health and a wish to feel younger. However, the introduction of Blood Pressure Insights led to a warning letter from the FDA, underscoring the delicate balance between wellness tools and regulated medical devices.

With approximately 2.7 million members across over 200 countries and significant growth in sales commitments, WHOOP continues its upward trajectory. A major fundraising round in March 2026 secured $575 million from a diverse group of investors, including sovereign wealth funds from the Gulf, U.S. health institutions like Mayo Clinic and Abbott, and prominent athletes. This influx of capital signals a shift from survival to a focus on global scale, research and development, and technological advancement.

Ahmed's advice to aspiring entrepreneurs is clear: start a company not for glamour, but out of an obsession with solving a problem. He emphasizes that a mission worth fighting for can empower founders to overcome incredibly difficult periods and emerge stronger on the other side.

item.title: Denis Shatalin - How to dominate your SaaS market through lowest cost per demo
item.description: The key to outperforming competitors in the SaaS market lies in achieving the lowest customer acquisition cost. By acquiring customers significantly cheaper than rivals, you gain an insurmountable adv...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: How to dominate your SaaS market through lowest cost per demo

Dominate Your SaaS Vertical: Booking the Cheapest Qualified Calls

The key to outperforming competitors in the SaaS market lies in achieving the lowest customer acquisition cost. By acquiring customers significantly cheaper than rivals, you gain an insurmountable advantage, regardless of their funding. This guide outlines a strategic approach to booking the most cost-effective qualified calls, a method that has helped clients book over 5,000 product demos and generate mid-eight figures in new sales.

Understanding and Articulating Core Pain Points

The foundational step to effective marketing is a deep understanding of the core pain your SaaS solution addresses. Without this clarity, efforts can become unfocused, leading to wasted ad spend, mismanaged tracking pixels, and the feeding of incorrect data.

First, clearly define the exact pain you alleviate for your Ideal Customer Profile (ICP). Then, meticulously detail the buyer persona experiencing this pain most acutely. This clarity is crucial for identifying the specific buying situations where this pain becomes most urgent. These situations form the bedrock of your initial marketing campaigns, ensuring your pixel data is accurate and providing a solid base for generating substantial revenue.

Identifying High-Urgency Buying Scenarios

Once you've identified your ICP and the pain you solve, brainstorm at least 10 distinct scenarios where your target audience would feel the most urgent need for your solution. This approach avoids the common pitfall of trying to educate or convince problem-unaware prospects. Instead, focus on reaching problem-aware and solution-aware buyers and simply articulating how your offering is the superior solution.

Consider an example: if your SaaS helps with end-of-year financial reporting, opportune buying situations include the end of the fiscal year or a few months prior. During these times, businesses are actively seeking solutions like yours. Appearing before them during this window shortens sales cycles and facilitates easier scaling.

Beyond temporal triggers, identify other situational cues. These could include:

  • Immediately after hiring a new accounting manager.
  • Following the dismissal of a junior accountant.
  • Other signals indicating a need for improved efficiency or expertise.

By correctly identifying these scenarios, you can deliver your message and offer at precisely the right moment, enabling you to win business not only from individual companies but also from larger enterprises.

Focusing Your Efforts: The Top Three Bets

From your list of 10 buying scenarios, pinpoint the top three that represent your strongest opportunities. A common mistake is to spread resources equally across all scenarios. Recognize that some situations will naturally lend themselves to easier conversions than others. These are your "top bets."

Aim to have three to four such high-conviction buying situations for your campaigns. These are scenarios where implementing your product should feel like a no-brainer for the prospect, leading to conversion within a single meeting.

Versatile Messaging and Ad Formats

With your top three scenarios identified, create multiple ad variations for each. In today's advertising landscape, particularly with updates like those from Meta, versatility in messaging, ad formats, and offer delivery is paramount. A lack of versatility can lead to budget burnout and frustration.

By articulating your offer effectively across these scenarios and presenting it in diverse ad formats, you provide the advertising platform with the necessary flexibility to discover the most performant angles.

For your top three bets, develop three to four distinct video ads each. For the remaining scenarios, create one or a few video ads. This strategy typically results in a campaign of 15 to 25 video ads. With a solid foundation, accurate pixel data, and well-articulated messaging, launching such a campaign with a modest ad budget (e.g., $5-10k) should yield a reasonable cost per call and effectively fill your pipeline.

If you encounter issues, it's likely due to incorrect offer articulation, an overly creative approach, or a flawed foundational setup.

Building for Repeatable Success

The ultimate strategy for booking the cheapest qualified calls and outperforming competitors lies in establishing a robust foundation for identifying winning ad angles repeatedly. Once you discover which angles perform best, leverage these insights to launch scaling campaigns. Continue this process until you have accumulated 8 to 15 distinct winning ad variations. Achieving this level of success makes hitting $1-3 million in ARR within a year highly probable.

For those seeking further insights into achieving these revenue milestones or wishing to bypass the trial-and-error process, resources are available. You can explore detailed breakdowns on a Telegram channel or apply to work directly with experts to accelerate your growth.

item.title: Bloomberg Originals - Inside the Mind of Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei | The Circuit | Extended Interview
item.description: The pace of artificial intelligence development feels like accelerating away from Earth at relativistic speeds. As Anthropic, a leading AI company, finds itself at the epicenter of this revolution, it...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Inside the Mind of Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei | The Circuit | Extended Interview

The Exponential Frontier: Navigating the AI Revolution with Dario Amodei

The pace of artificial intelligence development feels like accelerating away from Earth at relativistic speeds. As Anthropic, a leading AI company, finds itself at the epicenter of this revolution, its CEO, Dario Amodei, reflects on the dizzying speed of progress, the ethical considerations, and the future of technology.

Inside Anthropic: A Relativistic Pace

Amodei likens the current AI landscape to special relativity, where time seems to warp with increasing speed. "You go to sleep and you wake up in two days have gone by on earth. And so you have to deal with two days in one day," he explains, capturing the feeling of exponential acceleration. While the pressure is immense, Amodei emphasizes a need for rational, calm decision-making, akin to a surgeon or military officer, rather than succumbing to paranoia or complacency.

The demand for Anthropic's AI, Claude, is surging, even in the consumer space, despite the company's initial focus on enterprise solutions. This rapid growth has propelled Anthropic to the forefront of the AI universe, a position Amodei acknowledges with a mix of surprise and a recognition of the underlying "smooth exponential" trend he's observed throughout his career. The core questions remain the same: how to train good models, deploy them in beneficial products, ensure safety, and manage societal risks, all now under an amplified spotlight.

Dario Amodei's Journey: From Curiosity to AI Leadership

Growing up in San Francisco, Amodei's early interests lay in mathematics and understanding the universe, rather than the burgeoning tech scene. His parents, a leather craftsman and a librarian, fostered a spirit of curiosity. He credits the city's culture of nonconformity and individualism for influencing his worldview, encouraging the pursuit of ambitious, even unconventional, ideas.

Before co-founding Anthropic, Amodei was involved with the Open Philanthropy project, advising on biological research. This background likely informed his later focus on the potential of AI in areas like medicine and scientific discovery.

The Genesis of Anthropic: Leaving OpenAI

Amodei's departure from OpenAI is a significant event in Silicon Valley lore. He explains it not as a disagreement over specific safety issues, which are common in the field, but as a fundamental breakdown of trust. "When you feel that you can't trust someone, when you feel that their values are not what they say they are, when you feel that they're not honest," he states, "that makes it very hard to continue to work with a company." For Amodei, the resolution was to forge a new path, allowing both entities to pursue their distinct visions.

Navigating the AI Landscape: From India to Enterprise

A memorable moment at India's AI Summit, where Amodei and Sam Altman appeared to avoid holding hands, was attributed to the summit's disorganization rather than a personal rift. This incident, however, sparked broader questions about trust and cooperation among AI leaders. Amodei believes that while there's a variance in trustworthiness among AI builders, many actors are indeed trustworthy. He advocates for collaboration among these reliable entities to set industry standards, influencing those who might not otherwise adopt responsible practices.

Anthropic's strategic bet on coding and enterprise solutions, rather than solely focusing on consumer-facing applications, stemmed from a desire to align its business model with its core values. Amodei argues that enterprise applications, such as those in biotech, energy, education, and healthcare, offer more synergistic opportunities for positive impact and long-term trust-building compared to consumer models driven by engagement and advertising. This approach, he believes, minimizes conflicts between business goals and ethical principles.

The SaaS Apocalypse and the Future of Software

The rapid advancement of AI has led to significant market shifts, with traders dubbing recent events the "SaaSpocalypse." Amodei acknowledges that AI will undoubtedly disrupt traditional software. However, he believes that while some moats, like the ability to write complex code, will erode, others, such as customer relationships, domain knowledge, and unique insights, will become even more critical. His advice to software companies is to avoid complacency, identify their enduring strengths, and adapt to the evolving landscape. He predicts that the overall software industry will likely expand, even as some incumbents face significant challenges.

Partnerships and Valuations: Navigating Big Tech

Anthropic's substantial backing from tech giants like Amazon, Google, and Microsoft presents a complex dynamic of partnership and rivalry. Amodei asserts that Anthropic maintains its independence, citing instances where he has spoken out on critical issues, such as export controls to China, even when it might conflict with the interests of its partners. He emphasizes that these relationships are built on mutual benefit and the ability to disagree on certain matters while collaborating on others.

Regarding Anthropic's high valuation, Amodei explains it as a rational response to the rapid scaling of compute needs. While the company's fundamentals are strong, the unpredictable nature of AI development necessitates significant capital as a buffer against future uncertainties.

The Compute Crunch and the Race for Supremacy

The intense demand for computing power has led to reports of server strain and reliability issues. Amodei acknowledges the "compute crunch" but clarifies that Anthropic has been proactively planning for significant growth. While the company didn't anticipate the unprecedented revenue growth seen in early 2026, it has secured substantial compute deals and remains confident in its ability to acquire necessary resources. He views the current situation as a temporary, albeit extreme, surge rather than a sustained, unsustainable trend.

Amodei downplays the idea of "beating rivals" as the primary motivation. Instead, he sees Anthropic's leading position as an opportunity to "pull the ecosystem along," influencing the responsible development and deployment of AI. He believes that success is about more than just competition; it's about shaping the future of the technology for the collective good.

Staying True to Values Amidst Growth

As Anthropic scales, maintaining its core values becomes paramount. Amodei admits to being "paranoid at every scale" of the company's growth, recognizing that new challenges emerge at each stage. He dedicates significant time to cultivating Anthropic's culture, ensuring that new hires, particularly those from large tech companies, understand and adopt the company's unique operating principles. This cultural preservation, alongside product development, is a top priority for him and co-founder Daniela.

Product Velocity and AI Discoveries

Anthropic's remarkable product velocity is attributed to two key factors: a unified company culture and the use of Claude itself to accelerate model development and product creation. This synergy, Amodei explains, is producing increasingly reliable acceleration.

The most astonishing AI discoveries, according to Amodei, lie in biology and medicine. He recounts instances where Claude has diagnosed medical issues missed by human doctors and highlights the models' growing proficiency in drug design and computational chemistry. These advancements, he believes, will yield enormous benefits, improving human experience and accelerating scientific progress.

The Art of Writing and the Future of Work

Amodei uses Claude to assist in his writing, primarily for brainstorming and organizing thoughts, though he maintains creative control. He acknowledges the potential for AI to surpass human writing capabilities but emphasizes the importance of critical thinking and the struggle involved in the writing process. He worries about losing these benefits if AI is used as a complete end-to-end solution.

Regarding job displacement, Amodei has consistently warned about the potential for AI to eliminate a significant portion of entry-level white-collar jobs. While he stresses that his message is not one of doom but of proactive response, he remains concerned about the scale of disruption. He advocates for a multi-faceted approach, including economic policies, and acknowledges the need to find new roles for displaced workers, such as those in human-centered professions or roles that direct AI.

AI and Warfare: A Delicate Balance

Anthropic's involvement with the Department of Defense stems from a belief that the US needs to defend itself against resurgent authoritarian powers. Amodei supports strengthening US military capabilities to deter conflict, but draws a firm line against mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons, which he believes undermine democratic values. This principled stance has led to both collaboration and conflict with government entities.

Regarding specific military actions, Amodei clarifies that while Anthropic provides technology, the ultimate policy decisions rest with military leaders. He emphasizes that the company's role is to establish high-level boundaries that align with its values and promote those that encourage them. He expresses concern about potential mistakes in warfare, even with AI assistance, and highlights the critical importance of human oversight in decision-making.

Mythos: A Powerful Tool Requiring Caution

The development of Mythos, Anthropic's advanced AI model capable of autonomously navigating the cyber kill chain, has raised significant concerns. Amodei reveals that early testers described it as a "super weapon," prompting Anthropic to delay its public release. The goal is not to hoard the technology but to gradually open access with robust cyber safeguards, acknowledging that current defenses are not yet sufficient. He refutes claims that Mythos's capabilities can be easily replicated by open-source models, citing concrete examples of vulnerabilities discovered.

Amodei believes that by providing Mythos to defenders first, Anthropic can help patch existing vulnerabilities, ultimately leading to a more secure internet ecosystem. He criticizes the "sniping" and "cheap marketing" from other companies and individuals who he feels are not seriously engaging with the complex trade-offs involved.

The Question of Nationalization and Checks and Balances

Amodei views the question of government takeover of AI companies as serious. While he doesn't advocate for outright nationalization, he highlights the historical precedent of governments leading technological development. He believes that AI's development in the private sector, with government involvement coming late, is an unstable situation.

He champions a system of checks and balances, both within AI companies and between companies and the government. Anthropic's "long-term benefit trust" structure, which allows an external body to appoint and remove board members, is an example of introducing public governance elements. He calls for legislative and judicial branches to establish clear regulations and pre-release testing for AI models, advocating for a sensible, moderate approach rather than extreme reactions of either anti-regulation or complete government control.

The Future of AI: Recursive Self-Improvement and Civilization's Trajectory

Amodei sees AI's self-improvement as a continuous process rather than a singular event. He notes an accelerating exponential growth in AI capabilities, emphasizing the need for countermeasures to evolve in parallel. He cautions against extreme reactions, whether it's outright opposition to all AI regulation or calls for immediate shutdown, advocating instead for a measured, adaptive approach.

His favorite book, "The Making of the Atomic Bomb," leads him to identify with Leo Szilard, the visionary behind the nuclear chain reaction concept. Amodei believes that navigating the AI revolution requires a balance of power and checks and balances, rather than relying on singular, powerful figures. He views Oppenheimer's story as a cautionary tale of what should not happen.

Regarding the risk of civilizational collapse, Amodei acknowledges a 10-25% probability, stemming from the inherent unpredictability of the technology and the complex global landscape. He asserts that Anthropic's actions are aimed at lowering this probability, not increasing it, by developing safer AI and implementing robust defense mechanisms. He uses the analogy of an airline company: while a 25% chance of a crash is unacceptable, striving for a significantly safer airline is the goal.

Trust and the Path Forward

When asked why people should trust Anthropic, Amodei acknowledges that starting from a position of distrust is rational, given Silicon Valley's past actions. He argues that trust must be earned through concrete actions, citing Anthropic's commitment to its values, even when commercially disadvantageous, such as delaying product releases or cutting ties with certain markets. He asks for an evaluation of Anthropic's overall history, suggesting that the most consistent hypothesis is a genuine effort to do the right thing, despite being an imperfect organization.

Key Takeaways

  • Exponential Pace: AI development is accelerating at an unprecedented rate, requiring constant adaptation and rational decision-making.
  • Values-Driven Business: Anthropic prioritizes aligning its business model with its core values, focusing on enterprise applications for long-term impact.
  • Trust and Cooperation: Building trust among AI leaders is crucial for responsible development, with collaboration among trustworthy actors being key.
  • Navigating Disruption: AI will reshape the software industry, emphasizing the importance of enduring customer relationships and domain knowledge.
  • Responsible Deployment: Anthropic is committed to developing powerful AI while implementing safeguards, particularly in sensitive areas like cybersecurity and military applications.
  • Human Oversight: The principle of human decision-making in critical applications, especially warfare, is paramount.
  • Balanced Approach: Amodei advocates for a moderate, sensible approach to AI regulation, emphasizing checks and balances between companies and government.
  • Proactive Response: Warnings about AI's potential impacts, particularly on the workforce, are intended to spur proactive solutions rather than induce panic.
  • Continuous Improvement: AI's self-improvement is a continuous process, requiring ongoing assessment and adaptation of safety measures.
  • Earning Trust: Trust in AI companies must be earned through consistent actions that demonstrate a commitment to ethical principles and societal well-being.

item.title: Vietnam Innovators Digest - Emergency Podcast: The Iran War Impact on Vietnam | Michael Kokalari | EP 384
item.description: The global economy is facing unprecedented challenges as a major disruption in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for oil transport, threatens to send prices soaring and trigger widespread econom...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Emergency Podcast: The Iran War Impact on Vietnam | Michael Kokalari | EP 384

The Geopolitical Oil Shock: Impact on Vietnam and the Global Economy

The global economy is facing unprecedented challenges as a major disruption in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for oil transport, threatens to send prices soaring and trigger widespread economic hardship. This situation, exacerbated by geopolitical tensions, has significant implications for Vietnam, a nation increasingly positioning itself as a vital "connector economy."

The Strait of Hormuz Crisis and Global Ramifications

Approximately 20 million barrels of oil, representing about 17% of global daily demand, flow through the Strait of Hormuz. A disruption here has immediate and severe consequences. While current oil price increases are around 70-80%, historical precedents, like the 1973 oil crisis where a smaller supply disruption led to a quadrupling of prices, suggest the potential for far greater escalation.

The market's current relative calm is largely attributed to the expectation that former President Trump might de-escalate the situation, a phenomenon dubbed the "TACO paradox" (Trump Always Chickens Out paradox). However, if a de-escalation doesn't occur within the next two to three weeks, the global economy could face a hit more severe than the COVID-19 pandemic. This impact will not be limited to oil; disruptions to fertilizer, helium, and LNG supplies are also anticipated.

Vietnam's Navigational Strategy and Economic Resilience

Despite the global turmoil, Vietnam's government has demonstrated adeptness in navigating the crisis. Through diplomatic efforts, including a recent visit by the Prime Minister to Russia, Vietnam has secured additional oil supplies, potentially covering several more weeks. China has also contributed by supplying refined products to the region.

Crucially, the immediate impact on daily life in Vietnam has been less visible than in other nations. Unlike countries like the Philippines, which have declared national emergencies, or others that have closed schools, Vietnam has not experienced widespread disruptions. This is largely due to public confidence in the government's ability to manage the situation. While the overall economy will face challenges, Vietnam is expected to emerge from this period in a stronger position than many realize.

The K-Shaped Economy and Impact on Vietnamese Households

The current economic climate is likely to exacerbate the "K-shaped economy" phenomenon, where the upper-middle class fares relatively well, while the working class suffers disproportionately. In Vietnam, this is already evident with fertilizer prices doubling, placing significant strain on working families.

The cost of everyday goods is expected to rise. While the government has implemented tax cuts on petrol, diesel prices have nearly doubled. As fertilizer accounts for approximately 20% of food costs, inflation is projected to exceed 5% unless more drastic measures are taken. This inflation directly impacts those living on fixed salaries, as wages often lag behind rising prices. Businesses will need to adapt, but the primary concern lies with households whose purchasing power is eroded.

Gold Prices and Alternative Assets

In times of global uncertainty, gold has historically served as a safe haven. While initial reactions might see gold prices dip as people liquidate assets, a dramatic increase is anticipated. Some analysts predict gold prices could exceed $6,000 by the end of the year. Alternative assets like Bitcoin have also shown some resilience, though their long-term performance remains a subject of expert analysis. The underlying driver for the appreciation of alternative assets is the expectation that Western governments will resort to increased money printing to manage the economic fallout.

Vietnam's Energy Intensity and Vulnerability

Vietnam is a highly energy-intensive economy, with its energy consumption twice that of the Philippines and nearly three times that of Indonesia. This is partly due to the pervasive use of motorbikes for deliveries and transportation. While oil consumption decreased during severe lockdowns, it only saw a reduction of about 15%, highlighting the nation's reliance on energy. A forced reduction of 30% in oil consumption due to global supply constraints could lead to a significant contraction in GDP, potentially making the economic impact worse than COVID-19.

A substantial portion of Vietnam's oil is sourced from the Middle East, particularly for its heavy oil refinery financed by Kuwait and Japan. This dependence makes the Strait of Hormuz disruptions particularly impactful.

The Push for Renewables and Energy Security

In response to global energy security concerns, Vietnam has set ambitious goals to increase its renewable energy sources, aiming for 50% by 2050. Currently, renewables constitute around 10% of the energy mix, despite a recent surge in solar energy adoption. This push is partly driven by the desire to attract foreign investment, as companies like LEGO seek net-zero energy solutions. The development of direct power purchase agreements (DPPAs) allows factories to source electricity directly from solar farms, enabling them to meet sustainability targets for Western customers.

Historical Parallels: The Oil Shocks of the 1970s

The current crisis draws parallels to the oil shocks of 1973 and 1979, triggered by the Iran hostage crisis. In those instances, a 6-7% disruption in global oil supply led to a quadrupling of oil prices. The US, heavily reliant on oil imports at the time, experienced severe "stagflation" – a combination of high inflation and declining GDP growth. This historical context fuels concerns about a potential return to such an economic environment globally.

Vietnam's Strategic Petroleum Reserve and Refining Capacity

A key vulnerability for Vietnam is its limited Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), estimated to hold only about 10 days' worth of oil. This contrasts sharply with countries like Thailand (10 times Vietnam's reserve) and Japan (8 months). Building a larger SPR, increasing refining capacity, and continuing the push for renewable energy are crucial steps for enhancing Vietnam's energy security.

Currency Dynamics and Trade Balance

The global demand for dollars to purchase oil is strengthening the US dollar. Vietnam, a heavily export-oriented country, typically earns dollars through its trade surplus. However, the rising cost of oil imports is expected to lead to a trade deficit in the coming months, a significant shift from its usual surplus. This, coupled with potential depreciation pressure on the Vietnamese Dong, could lead to higher interest rates, impacting GDP growth.

LNG as a Solution and US Supply Advantage

Vietnam has been advised to ramp up Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) purchases, particularly from the US. The US has a significant advantage in LNG supply due to "associated gas" – a byproduct of oil exploration – which is sometimes priced at zero or even negative due to storage limitations. This abundance makes US LNG a viable and strategic option for Vietnam to address its energy needs and balance its trade with the US.

The Future of Oil Prices and Economic Recovery

Even if the current conflict de-escalates within weeks, the oil market is unlikely to return to pre-crisis levels immediately. The shutdown of oil wells ("shut-ins") will take months to reverse, and global demand is expected to be higher than pre-war levels as countries rebuild their strategic reserves. Analysts predict that the average oil price for the year could still hover around $87 per barrel, a significant increase from previous years.

Inflation and the Everyday Vietnamese Person

The projected inflation rate of up to 5.5% and a potential reduction in GDP growth will have tangible effects on the average Vietnamese citizen. For those with lower incomes, rising prices for essentials like coffee, transportation, and food will mean real hardship. The government is expected to implement more substantial subsidies and potentially trim personal income and VAT taxes to support vulnerable households.

Key Takeaways

  • Global Economic Shock: Disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz pose a significant threat to global oil supply, with potential for severe economic consequences exceeding those of the COVID-19 pandemic.
  • Vietnam's Resilience: Despite global challenges, Vietnam's government has implemented measures to secure energy supplies, and the country is poised to emerge stronger from the crisis.
  • Inflationary Pressures: Rising fertilizer and fuel costs will lead to increased inflation, disproportionately affecting working-class households in Vietnam.
  • Energy Security Imperative: The crisis highlights the need for Vietnam to bolster its Strategic Petroleum Reserve, expand refining capacity, and accelerate its transition to renewable energy.
  • Geopolitical Shifts: The current geopolitical landscape positions Vietnam as a crucial "connector economy," benefiting from its balanced relationships with global powers.
  • Historical Context: Past oil shocks serve as a stark reminder of the potential for stagflation and severe economic downturns.

item.title: The Artificial Intelligence Show Podcast - Ep. 220: AI Answers - The 2026 State of AI for Business Report
item.description: 0:00 · I sit in meetings all day long as a leader of our organization where I'm hearing things that I didn't tell people to do. I didn't put it in place like go do this, go pursue this because we're e...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Ep. 220: AI Answers - The 2026 State of AI for Business Report

Transcript

Intro

0:00 · I sit in meetings all day long as a leader of our organization where I'm hearing things that I didn't tell people to do. I didn't put it in place like go do this, go pursue this because we're empowering AI forward professionals.

0:13 · They every day are challenging the traditional way of doing things and say well what about this? Why can't we just build an app for that? Why can't I build a skill for this? And so like once that happens then all of the sudden you start looking at people like you're not even doing the role you were hired for anymore. Like you're literally functioning in this like whole new role that we almost have to like rewrite your job description.

0:35 · [music] Welcome to AI Answers, a special Q&A series from the Artificial Intelligence Show. [music] I'm Paul Ritzer, founder and CEO of Smarter X and Marketing AI Institute. Every time we host our live virtual events and online classes, we get dozens of great questions [music] from business leaders and practitioners who are navigating this fastmoving world of AI. But we never have enough time to get to all of them. So we created the AI answers series to [music] address more of these questions and share real time insights into the topics and challenges professionals like you are facing.
